items.hook: Supplement that binds to microplastics may remove them from our body
items.body: A supplement comprised of dead bacteria seems to remove microplastics, and prevent their absorption into cells, by attracting them to the bacteria's rough surfaces

items.hook: Nuclear weapons testing: The "Small Boy" test shot Little Feller I becomes the last atmospheric test detonation at the Nevada National Security Site.
items.body: Nuclear weapons tests are experiments carried out to determine the performance of nuclear weapons and the effects of their explosion. Worth a quiet trip down the rabbit hole.

items.hook: George Tan Soon-gin was a Hong Kong financial criminal and businessman and who was known for his role and involvement in the Carrian Group and the scandal that led to its collapse.
items.body: One of those things you can't un-know.

items.hook: Twelve inhabitants of Scillium (near Kasserine, modern-day Tunisia) in North Africa are executed for being Christians. This is the earliest record of Christianity in that part of the world.
items.body: The Scillitan Martyrs were a group of early Christians of North Africa executed by the Roman Empire in Carthage, modern Tunisia, on 17 July 180 AD. Worth a quiet trip down the rabbit hole.

items.hook: The form of government in the Republic of Finland is officially confirmed. For this reason, July 17 is known as the Day of Democracy (Kansanvallan päivä) in Finland.
items.body: Finland, officially the Republic of Finland, is a Nordic country in Northern Europe. Worth a quiet trip down the rabbit hole.

items.hook: Four year-old Henry IV is crowned as king of Germany in Aachen.
items.body: Henry IV was Holy Roman Emperor (1084–1105), King of Germany (1054–1105), and King of Italy and Burgundy (1056–1105). The world is stranger than it lets on.

items.hook: Legal Positivism is a book by the Italian jurist Norberto Bobbio about one of the ontological elements of foundations of law — the jusphilosophical school called juspositivism or legal positivism.

items.hook: In addition to its role in mythology, it may also have referred to real-world places at different times.
items.body: Vourukasha, also known as Warkash or Fraxkard, is the world ocean in Zoroastrian cosmology and Iranian mythology. One of those things you can't un-know.

items.hook: The Nieuport Triplanes were a series of experimental stagger-wing triplanes modified from Nieuport 10, 17 and 17bis biplanes during World War I that were built by Nieuport.
items.body: The exact number built is unknown, but only three or four are known to have been evaluated by the French Army Aviation and the British Royal Flying Corps (RFC) and Royal Naval Air Service (RNAS). Bring it up somewhere. Watch the reaction.

items.hook: Members of the French National Guard under the command of General Lafayette open fire on a crowd of radical Jacobins at the Champ de Mars, Paris, during the French Revolution, killing scores of people.
items.body: The National Guard is a French military, gendarmerie, and police reserve force, active in its current form since 2016 but originally founded in 1789 during the French Revolution. The world is stranger than it lets on.

items.hook: Oscar-winning actress Brenda Fricker dies aged 81
items.body: The Irish actress won the best supporting Oscar for ’My Left Foot’ in 1990.
